prompt: The passage is structured mainly as

choices.text: a comparison of two different cities Tomas has lived in
choices.rationale: Two cities are not compared in detail.

choices.text: a back-and-forth argument between Tomas and a friend
choices.rationale: There is no argument between characters.

choices.text: a numbered list of tips for writing a letter home
choices.rationale: No tips are listed.

choices.text: a sequence of three drafts leading to a final realization
choices.rationale: Correct. It moves through the three versions to Tomas's closing insight.

explanation: The arc runs through three drafts to a final realization.

stimulus.title: The Unsent Letter
stimulus.text: Tomas wrote the letter three times before he stopped trying to send it. In the first version he was cheerful, listing the small triumphs of his new city as if homesickness were a thing he had outgrown. In the second he was honest, and the page filled with everything he missed until it embarrassed him. The third version said almost nothing at all. He kept that one folded in a drawer. Months later he found it again and realized the words he could not send had still done their work: writing them had let him admit, if only to himself, how far from home he truly felt.

prompt: Which choice best describes how the passage is organized?

choices.text: It gives instructions for restoring a marsh that has been drained.
choices.rationale: No restoration instructions are given.

choices.text: It compares two different marshes located on opposite coasts.
choices.rationale: No two marshes are compared.

choices.text: It narrates the personal story of one person who lived by a marsh.
choices.rationale: No personal story is told.

choices.text: It overturns a first impression by detailing the marsh's hidden benefits.
choices.rationale: Correct. It begins with the marsh looking like wasteland, then reveals its value.

explanation: The passage moves from a dismissive first impression to the marsh's real worth.

stimulus.title: The Quiet Buffer
stimulus.text: Where the land meets the sea, a salt marsh can look like wasteland: flat, muddy, and crossed by winding channels. Yet this drab landscape does remarkable work. Its dense grasses slow incoming waves, blunting the force of storms before they reach homes inland. The same grasses trap sediment, letting the marsh build itself upward as the sea slowly rises. Beneath the mud, roots store carbon that might otherwise warm the air. For years, marshes were drained and filled without much thought. Only later did people recognize that the unglamorous marsh had been protecting them all along, asking nothing in return.

prompt: The passage develops its main idea primarily by

choices.text: comparing two competing symbols once used to represent zero
choices.rationale: No two zero symbols are compared.

choices.text: contrasting the older discomfort with zero against its later usefulness
choices.rationale: Correct. It sets the early unease about zero against its eventual power.

choices.text: listing the names of the mathematicians who first studied zero
choices.rationale: No mathematicians are named.

choices.text: providing a worked example of multiplying two large numbers
choices.rationale: No worked multiplication example appears.

explanation: The structure contrasts old discomfort with later usefulness.

stimulus.title: The Number That Was Nothing
stimulus.text: For much of history, there was no symbol for nothing. People counted sheep and coins easily enough, but the idea of writing down an empty amount seemed pointless, even unsettling. The breakthrough came when mathematicians treated zero not as the absence of a number but as a number in its own right, one that could be added, subtracted, and used to hold a place in larger figures. With zero, the difference between 5 and 50 could be written clearly, and long calculations grew far simpler. A concept that once felt like a riddle became the quiet hinge on which much of modern mathematics turns.

prompt: The example of the moth's wing versus a still leaf mainly serves to

choices.text: explain why leaves make better food for bats than insects do
choices.rationale: Bats do not eat leaves; the leaf is a contrast object.

choices.text: prove that moths are the only prey that bats are able to catch
choices.rationale: The passage does not limit bat prey to moths.

choices.text: illustrate how finely a bat can distinguish objects by echo
choices.rationale: Correct. The contrast shows the precision of echo-based perception.

choices.text: list the steps a bat follows when building its nighttime roost
choices.rationale: No roost-building steps are given.

explanation: The moth-versus-leaf example demonstrates the precision of echolocation.

stimulus.title: Seeing With Sound
stimulus.text: A bat hunting in pitch darkness does not bump into things. It calls out, often at pitches too high for humans to hear, and listens for the echoes that bounce back. From the delay between call and echo, the bat judges how far away an object is; from subtle shifts in the returning sound, it can tell whether the object is moving toward it or away. A moth's fluttering wing returns a different echo than a still leaf. So precise is this sense that some bats can snatch an insect from the air in total darkness. The world the bat perceives is built entirely of sound, a picture painted with echoes.

prompt: The final sentence mainly serves to

choices.text: argue that the spice voyages should never have happened
choices.rationale: The passage does not argue the voyages were a mistake.

choices.text: give step-by-step directions for harvesting black pepper
choices.rationale: No harvesting directions appear.

choices.text: introduce a new spice that had not been mentioned earlier
choices.rationale: No new spice is introduced at the end.

choices.text: note an irony in which success undid the rarity it depended on
choices.rationale: Correct. It highlights the irony that abundance dissolved the fortunes built on rarity.

explanation: The closing sentence delivers the irony of rarity undone by success.

stimulus.title: The Price of Pepper
stimulus.text: There was a time when a handful of black pepper could rival a handful of silver. The spice grew only in distant lands, and by the time it reached a European market it had passed through many hands, each adding a markup. Merchants guarded their routes jealously and spread tall tales about how spices were harvested, partly to discourage rivals. The high prices drove sailors to seek their own sea paths to the source, voyages that redrew the map of the world. In the end, the very abundance those voyages produced made spices ordinary, and the fortunes built on rarity quietly dissolved.

prompt: The passage is organized mainly by

choices.text: listing, step by step, how to identify a lichen in the field
choices.rationale: No identification steps are given.

choices.text: arguing that bare rock should be protected from all disturbance
choices.rationale: The passage makes no protection argument.

choices.text: comparing two rival kinds of lichen growing on the same stone
choices.rationale: No two lichens are compared.

choices.text: describing the lichen, its action on rock, and the succession it enables
choices.rationale: Correct. It moves from what a lichen is, to how it breaks rock, to later plants.

explanation: The structure runs from description to action to ecological succession.

stimulus.title: First on the Rock
stimulus.text: When a bare rock is left exposed, the first life to arrive is often a lichen, a strange partnership of fungus and alga living as one. The fungus grips the stone and gathers moisture; the alga, sheltered inside, makes food from sunlight. Together they need no soil at all. As a lichen grows, it slowly releases acids that crack the rock surface, and its dead tissue mixes with grit to form the first thin crumbs of soil. Years later, mosses and small plants can take root in that beginning. The lichen will likely be crowded out by the very community it made possible, an unsung founder of the green world that follows.

prompt: The author compares a series of locks to a staircase mainly to

choices.text: prove that climbing stairs is harder than operating a lock
choices.rationale: The passage does not compare difficulty of stairs and locks.

choices.text: list the materials needed to construct a single lock chamber
choices.rationale: The comparison is not a materials list.

choices.text: help readers picture how boats move up or down in steps
choices.rationale: Correct. The staircase image clarifies the step-by-step rise and fall of boats.

choices.text: argue that staircases should be built beside every river
choices.rationale: No argument about building staircases appears.

explanation: The staircase analogy is an explanatory image for stepwise elevation change.

stimulus.title: Stairs for Boats
stimulus.text: Rivers do not run level, and for centuries that simple fact blocked boats from traveling far inland. The lock changed things. A lock is a chamber with gates at each end; a boat enters, the gates close, and water is let in or drained out until the boat rises or falls to the next stretch of river. String several locks together, and a vessel can climb a hillside the way a person climbs a staircase. The engineering is humble, relying on nothing more exotic than gravity and patience, yet it knit distant towns into trading partners and turned shallow rivers into highways of commerce.

prompt: Which choice best describes the overall structure of the passage?

choices.text: It lists the steps a diver should take to collect a coral sample safely.
choices.rationale: No collection steps are given.

choices.text: It argues that coral reefs are endangered and must be legally protected.
choices.rationale: The passage makes no protection argument.

choices.text: It compares coral to a familiar record, explains the method, then notes a difficulty.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage moves from a tree-ring analogy to the band-reading method to a closing challenge.

choices.text: It traces the life cycle of a single coral colony from birth to death.
choices.rationale: It does not trace a colony's life cycle.

explanation: The passage proceeds from analogy to method to remaining difficulty.

stimulus.title: The Coral That Keeps Time
stimulus.text: A coral skeleton grows in fine bands, much like the rings of a tree. In bright, warm months the coral lays down thick layers; in cooler, dimmer months the layers thin. By slicing a coral and counting these bands, researchers can read a record of past seasons stretching back centuries. Even better, the chemistry trapped in each band hints at the water's temperature when it formed. So a single old coral can serve as a kind of diary, written not in words but in stone, recording conditions long before anyone thought to measure them. The challenge is learning to read a language no one designed to be read.
